compiled_state.title: Create a process to remediate legacy code
compiled_state.description:
## Context
Developers running DSO on nava-platform projects experience friction from accumulated codebase bloat that prevention gates cannot address retroactively. Specifically: false test failures from change-detector tests slow CI and erode trust in the test suite; stale documentation sections generate noise in code review; and dead code (unreachable branches, unused variables) slows codebase comprehension for new contributors. This epic adds a /dso:remediate skill that safely prunes high-confidence bloat patterns and surfaces lower-confidence candidates through an opus blue-team filter before presenting them for human resolution. The observable improvement: after running /dso:remediate --all, the developer sees a structured summary of what was cleaned, flagged, or skipped — and the next CI run, code review, and onboarding session encounters less noise.

Pattern namespace: bright-line patterns use category prefixes (T, D, C) and are eligible for auto-remediation. Probabilistic patterns use the P- prefix (P-T1, P-D3, P-C1) and are always routed to the blue-team agent when in confidence range — never auto-remediated regardless of threshold.

## Success Criteria

SC1. A new skill /dso:remediate is added, invoked with one of four scoped flags: --tests, --docs, --code, or --all. Invocation without a flag prints usage and exits non-zero. A sub-agent guard runs at skill entry: if the Agent tool is unavailable, the skill returns an error message and exits without mutations. Before any mutation, the skill checks for staged changes (git diff --cached --quiet); if staged changes exist, it aborts with an error.

SC2. A static confidence mapping file (YAML) ships with the plugin at plugins/dso/config/remediate-confidence-map.yaml, declaring {pattern_id, engine} -> confidence_score for every pattern/engine combination. Each per-stack linter (ruff, eslint, rubocop) has its own engine entry. Two new dso-config.conf keys control routing: remediate.auto_threshold (default: 90) and remediate.blue_team_threshold (default: 60). At runtime, the skill detects installed engines via command -v, looks up the highest-confidence installed engine for each pattern, and routes: >= auto_threshold -> auto-remediate, >= blue_team_threshold -> route to blue-team agent, < blue_team_threshold -> skip with a note in the summary.

SC3. The skill detects and auto-remediates the following bright-line patterns when confidence meets the auto threshold:
- --tests: T1 (assertion-less test body — auto-delete the test function), T2 (exact-duplicate test — auto-delete the duplicate), T3 (import-only test file — auto-delete the file)
- --docs: D2 (broken internal links — annotate with TODO: fix or remove inline comment), D4 (duplicate headings — included in summary as flagged_for_review with file path), D5 (large unstructured file — included in summary as flagged_for_review with file path)
- --code: C1 (unreachable branch after unconditional return/raise/throw — auto-delete the dead block), C2 (unused local variable without suppression annotation — auto-delete), C3 (exact-duplicate function body — included in summary as flagged_for_review with both file paths)
For C2, auto-prune is suppressed if the line contains # noqa, # type: ignore, or # side-effect; such matches are flagged for human review instead. For C1 and C2, the skill delegates to per-stack linters when available, falling back to semgrep rules.

SC4. For probabilistic patterns where the best available engine's confidence falls between the two thresholds, the skill dispatches a dso:bloat-blue-team opus sub-agent. Engine selection follows the same rule as SC2: the highest-confidence installed engine determines the routing decision. The blue-team agent emits one of three verdicts per candidate: CONFIRM, DISMISS (with rationale), or NEEDS_HUMAN (with ambiguity note). DISMISS candidates are excluded from the user-facing report. CONFIRM and NEEDS_HUMAN candidates both proceed to SC5 resolution paths. NEEDS_HUMAN is counted as a distinct field in the SC6 summary. Probabilistic patterns: P-T1 (change-detector test), P-D3 (stale example referencing absent symbol), P-C1 (feature flag never toggled to true). If no installed engine covers a probabilistic pattern, it is skipped and recorded in skipped_pattern_ids.

SC5. After blue-team filtering, the skill presents the user with a choice of two resolution paths:
- Path A (manual): Displays a numbered list of CONFIRM and NEEDS_HUMAN candidates with file location, pattern ID, and blue-team note. User enters item numbers to remove (comma-separated), or presses Enter to skip all. Fixture tests simulate Path A by injecting a pre-set selection list via DSO_REMEDIATE_SELECT env var.
- Path B (auto-resolve): Dispatches a dso:bloat-resolver opus sub-agent that applies all CONFIRM candidates and emits NO_CHANGE: <reason> for any it declines. Changes from the resolver follow the normal commit workflow (COMMIT-WORKFLOW.md -> /dso:review), ensuring at least two independent agents review every change.

SC6. The skill emits a structured session-end summary written to $ARTIFACTS_DIR/remediate-summary-<timestamp>.json with schema: {"auto_remediated": 0, "blue_team_dismissed": 0, "blue_team_needs_human": 0, "skipped_below_threshold": 0, "skipped_pattern_ids": [], "flagged_for_review": 0, "user_resolved": 0, "resolver_applied": 0, "resolver_no_change": 0, "flags": ["--tests"]}

SC7. Validation is run against each nava-platform template (flask, nextjs, rails, jekyll-uswds) using synthetic test fixtures at tests/fixtures/remediate/<template>/. Each fixture includes a fixture-manifest.json declaring expected counts and file-level outcomes. Validation asserts: auto-remediated items -> target file/function absent and auto_remediated count matches; flagged items -> no mutation and flagged_for_review count matches; skipped items -> skipped_pattern_ids contains expected IDs. Each fixture also includes at least one valid-but-pattern-matching item (e.g., intentional golden-file test, changelog with duplicate headings) that must NOT be removed — the manifest declares these as expected_false_positive_safe and the test asserts they remain unmodified.

Out of scope: P-D4 (concept drift), P-D1 (orphaned sections), P-T2 (mock-heavy tests), P-C2 (redundant parameters). These are deferred to follow-on epic bc7f-1a0d.

## Scenario Analysis
- P-T1 golden-file false positives (critical): Intentional regression tests are structurally identical to change-detectors; the static confidence mapping cannot distinguish them by project context.
- P-C1 env-flag false positives (critical): Feature flags gated on environment variables are statically never true during analysis, matching every env-conditional flag.
- auto_threshold=0 bypass (critical): Setting threshold to 0 routes all matches to auto-prune, bypassing blue-team entirely.
- Unbounded resolver batch (important): 50+ auto-prune candidates in a single dispatch may exhaust agent context.
- Test coverage gap after deletion (important): Deleting the sole test covering a source file leaves an invisible coverage gap.
- Interrupted mid-batch state (important): No checkpoint/state file to resume from after SIGURG.
- Inverted thresholds dead zone (important): blue_team_threshold > auto_threshold creates undefined routing behavior.
- Missing config key (important): Absent threshold keys cause bash comparison failure and silent abort.
- Stack-detection mismatch (important): Monorepo stack detection may select wrong linter for confidence lookup.

## Prompt Alignment Findings
- Blue-team agent must form its verdict before consulting the engine confidence score to avoid anchoring
- Blue-team should default to DISMISS when uncertain — false CONFIRMs are amplified by the resolver; false DISMISSes are recoverable
- Resolver prompt must NOT mention the downstream review gate — prevents moral hazard
- Resolver must check for callers before deleting any function/class/export and emit NO_CHANGE with CASCADE_RISK when callers exist outside the candidate list
- Embed these as hard constraints in agent definition files during implementation

## Research Findings

### Code Bloat Remediation
- Polyglot Piranha (Uber, https://github.com/uber/piranha): Java/Kotlin/Swift/ObjC/JS/Go. Tree-sitter AST with cascading cleanup rules. Auto-remediates (210K lines deleted, 1611 PRs). Binary match/no-match — no confidence tiering. Excels at feature flag cascading cleanup.
- DCE-LLM (NAACL 2025, https://aclanthology.org/2025.naacl-long.501/): Hybrid CodeBERT triage + LLM judgment. 94%+ F1. Architecturally closest to our approach but requires fine-tuned models.
- Knip (https://github.com/webpro-nl/knip): JS/TS only. TypeScript type checker as ground truth. Highest-precision JS/TS dead code detector — candidate linter delegate for JS/TS stack.
- Semgrep autofix (https://semgrep.dev/docs/writing-rules/autofix): 20+ languages. Rule-based or LLM-assisted (https://choly.ca/post/semgrep-autofix-llm/). No native confidence tiering.
- Datadog: LLM false-positive filtering for static analysis (https://www.datadoghq.com/blog/using-llms-to-filter-out-false-positives/)

### Documentation Bloat Remediation
- lychee (https://github.com/lycheeverse/lychee): Async Rust link checker. Fast, caching, .lycheeignore. Report only.
- markdownlint (https://github.com/DavidAnson/markdownlint): MD024 duplicate headings with siblings_only option (changelog-aware). Has auto-fix. More precise than bash-grep for D4.
- Doc Detective (https://github.com/doc-detective): Execution-based stale example detection (runs docs against live system). Complementary to LLM approach.
- Vale (https://github.com/errata-ai/vale): Prose style linter with YAML rules. Can approximate P-D3 with deprecated-term lists but brittle vs LLM approach.

### Test Bloat Remediation
- TSDetect (https://github.com/TestSmells/TSDetect): Java only. 20 test smells including assertion-less (Unknown Test). Report only.
- TestIQ (https://github.com/pydevtools/TestIQ): Python/pytest. Coverage-based duplicate detection. Higher FP rate than AST hashing.
- UTRefactor (FSE 2025, https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.16739): Java. LLM-based auto-refactoring of test smells (89% reduction). Closest to our blue-team filter but collapses detection and remediation into one pass.
- Mutation testing (PIT, mutmut): Ground truth for test redundancy but too slow for commit gates.

### Key Tradeoff Summary
No existing tool implements all three of our architectural pillars: per-pattern confidence mapping, tiered routing (auto/LLM/skip), and per-stack linter delegation. Implementation should consider: Knip as JS/TS linter delegate, markdownlint for D4 (siblings_only precision), Piranha's cascading pattern for resolver design.

## Dependencies
No blocking epic dependencies. The confidence mapping YAML (plugins/dso/config/remediate-confidence-map.yaml) is a deliverable artifact — its schema is a stable interface that follow-on epic bc7f-1a0d will consume. Two new dso-config.conf entries: remediate.auto_threshold, remediate.blue_team_threshold. Runtime tool availability handled by confidence mapping graceful degradation.

## Approach
Option A: Tiered Engine Selection. Ship a static confidence mapping YAML declaring {pattern_id, engine} -> confidence_score. At runtime, detect available engines via command -v, look up the highest-confidence available engine for each pattern, and route through three tiers using two global config thresholds. Detection engines: semgrep (declarative AST rules, polyglot), ast-grep (structural queries), bash-grep (textual fallback), plus delegation to per-stack linters (ruff, eslint, rubocop) where they already detect the pattern.

## Resolved Implementation Gaps

### Gap 1: Agent Contracts (resolved 2026-04-14)
**dso:bloat-blue-team input**: {candidates: [{candidate_id, pattern_id, file, line_range, excerpt, engine, confidence}]}
**dso:bloat-blue-team output**: {verdicts: [{candidate_id, verdict, rationale, rationale_basis}]}
- rationale required for DISMISS and NEEDS_HUMAN, optional for CONFIRM
- rationale_basis enum: excerpt_only | file_context | cross_file (required)
- Contract file: plugins/dso/docs/contracts/bloat-blue-team-payload.md

**dso:bloat-resolver input**: {confirmed_candidates: [{candidate_id, pattern_id, file, line_range, action}]}
- action enum: delete_function | delete_file | delete_block | annotate | flag
**dso:bloat-resolver output**: {applied: [{candidate_id, action_taken}], no_change: [{candidate_id, category, reason}]}
- no_change.category enum: CASCADE_RISK | INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT | PATTERN_UNCLEAR | STACK_UNCERTAINTY (required)

### Gap 2: Config Validation Behavior (resolved 2026-04-14)
Add to SC2: If either config key (remediate.auto_threshold, remediate.blue_team_threshold) is absent, non-numeric, or outside 1-100, log warning to stderr naming the invalid key and value, then use defaults (auto=90, blue_team=60) for the entire run. If blue_team_threshold >= auto_threshold after resolution, log warning (inverted thresholds) but proceed.

### Gap 3: Monorepo Multi-Stack Support (resolved 2026-04-14)
Add to SC2: At scan start, classify files in scope into language groups by extension. For each language group, select the highest-confidence installed engine. Extension-to-language mapping is a static lookup table shipped alongside the confidence mapping YAML. Unrecognized extensions scanned with bash-grep only.

### Gap 4: Prompt Engineering Constraints (resolved 2026-04-14)
Blue-team: form verdict before consulting confidence; default to DISMISS when uncertain; 80%+ definitive rate; declare rationale_basis.
Resolver: no mention of downstream review gate; check callers before deleting; categorize every NO_CHANGE.

### Gap 5: Feasibility Spike Results (resolved 2026-04-14)
SC3 ACHIEVABLE on all 4 stacks. Confidence ranges: Python python-ast 95, semgrep 85; JS/TS ast-grep 88; Ruby ast-grep 85; Markdown bash-grep 90. Ruby C1 lower confidence — consider routing to blue-team.

Converted from original ticket w21-bsnz (enriched via /dso:brainstorm). Follow-on epic bc7f-1a0d created for 5 advanced patterns (P-D4, P-D1, P-T2, P-C2, P-C1 upgrade) blocked by this epic.

### Planning Intelligence Log
- Web research (Step 2.6): triggered, then re-triggered via gate (user-requested polyglot remediation research)
  - Bright-line conditions that fired: none (initial); user-request trigger (re-run)
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 9 of 16
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: web research re-run 1 time(s)
- Follow-on scrutiny (Step 0): triggered — depth: 0 (stub, user-requested pause before decomposition)
- Feasibility resolution (Step 2.5): not triggered
- LLM-instruction signal (Step 5): triggered — keyword: skill file modifications
comments.author: Test

comments.body:
## Brainstorm Gap Resolutions

### Gap 1: Agent Contracts (3da0-dc8c) — RESOLVED

**dso:bloat-blue-team input**: {candidates: [{candidate_id, pattern_id, file, line_range, excerpt, engine, confidence}]}
**dso:bloat-blue-team output**: {verdicts: [{candidate_id, verdict, rationale, rationale_basis}]}
- rationale required for DISMISS and NEEDS_HUMAN, optional for CONFIRM
- rationale_basis enum: excerpt_only | file_context | cross_file (required)

**dso:bloat-resolver input**: {confirmed_candidates: [{candidate_id, pattern_id, file, line_range, action}]}
- action enum: delete_function | delete_file | delete_block | annotate | flag
**dso:bloat-resolver output**: {applied: [{candidate_id, action_taken}], no_change: [{candidate_id, category, reason}]}
- no_change.category enum: CASCADE_RISK | INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT | PATTERN_UNCLEAR | STACK_UNCERTAINTY (required)

### Gap 2: Config Validation (93f9-de68) — RESOLVED

Add to SC2: If either config key is absent, non-numeric, or outside 1-100, log warning to stderr naming the invalid key and value, then use defaults (auto=90, blue_team=60) for the entire run. If blue_team_threshold >= auto_threshold after resolution, log warning (inverted thresholds — all patterns route to blue-team or skip; no auto-remediation will occur) but proceed — this is a valid configuration that disables auto-remediation.

### Gap 3: Monorepo Multi-Stack (94f0-e142) — RESOLVED

Add to SC2: At scan start, classify files in scope into language groups by extension (.py -> python, .js/.ts/.tsx/.jsx -> javascript, .rb -> ruby, .md -> markdown). For each language group, select the highest-confidence installed engine from the confidence mapping. One-time classification at startup, not per-file. Extension-to-language mapping is a static lookup table shipped alongside the confidence mapping YAML. Unrecognized extensions scanned with bash-grep only. Replaces single-stack detection for remediation.

### Gap 4: Prompt Engineering Constraints (d64a-bd80) — RESOLVED

Formalized as testable behavioral constraints in agent contracts:

**Blue-team constraints:**
1. MUST form verdict before consulting confidence field (present candidates WITHOUT confidence in first pass)
2. MUST default to DISMISS when uncertain (false CONFIRMs amplified downstream; false DISMISSes recoverable)
3. MUST achieve >=80% definitive verdict rate (CONFIRM + DISMISS); NEEDS_HUMAN reserved for genuinely insufficient context
4. MUST declare rationale_basis (excerpt_only | file_context | cross_file)

**Resolver constraints:**
1. Prompt MUST NOT mention downstream review gate (prevents moral hazard)
2. MUST search for callers before deleting function/class/export; emit NO_CHANGE with CASCADE_RISK if callers found
3. MUST categorize every NO_CHANGE decision (CASCADE_RISK | INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT | PATTERN_UNCLEAR | STACK_UNCERTAINTY)

### Gap 5: Feasibility Spike (a8d5-423d) — RESOLVED

Smoke test on nava-platform templates (flask, nextjs, rails; jekyll repo unavailable):
- T1 (assertion-less test): DETECTED on Python (AST 95%), JS/TS (ast-grep+grep 90%), Ruby (ast-grep+grep 90%)
- T3 (import-only file): DETECTED on Python (AST 99%), JS/TS (grep 95%), Ruby (grep 90%)
- C1 (unreachable branch): DETECTED on Python (AST 95%), JS/TS (grep 80%), Ruby (grep 75% — implicit returns complicate)
- C2 (unused variable): DETECTED on Python (AST 95%), JS/TS (grep 70%), Ruby (grep 65%)
- Jekyll: only docs patterns (D2, D4, D5) apply — confirmed bash-grep sufficient (90%)

SC3 VERDICT: ACHIEVABLE on all 4 stacks. No SC changes needed.

Key spike finding: Python AST module provides highest confidence across all patterns. The confidence mapping should include python-ast as an engine alongside semgrep/ast-grep/bash-grep. For Ruby, C1 confidence is lower due to implicit returns and guard clauses — implementation should consider routing Ruby C1 matches to blue-team rather than auto-remediation.

Preliminary confidence ranges for the YAML:
- Python: python-ast 95 > semgrep 85 > bash-grep 60
- JS/TS: ast-grep+post 88 > bash-grep 65
- Ruby: ast-grep+post 85 > bash-grep 55
- Jekyll/Markdown: bash-grep 90 (docs patterns only)
comments.author: Test

comments.body:
## Agent Definition Skeletons (created during brainstorm)

Two agent definition files were created during brainstorm with HARD-GATE behavioral constraints informed by bot-psychologist analysis and prior-art research across 17 open-source projects (Anthropic security review, GitHub Security Lab taskflows, SWE-agent, Sweep, PR-Agent, refactor-clean, and others).

### plugins/dso/agents/bloat-blue-team.md
Skeleton with 6 HARD-GATEs: confidence-blind (agent never sees static confidence scores — orchestrator retains them for routing/audit), asymmetric-error-policy (DISMISS when uncertain), needs-human-floor (80% definitive verdict rate), scope-anchoring (classify only the excerpt), confirm-requires-evidence (bloat_evidence field required for CONFIRM), plus a trusted-by-default enumeration (snapshot tests, env-gated flags, changelogs auto-DISMISS).

Input/output contracts defined. Pattern-specific guidance for P-T1, P-D3, P-C1 included with CONFIRM/DISMISS signals and common false-positive traps.

**Implementation must:** fill in pattern-specific heuristics marked with {Implementation agents:} placeholders. Must NOT weaken, remove, or contradict any HARD-GATE section.

### plugins/dso/agents/bloat-resolver.md
Skeleton with 7 HARD-GATEs: finality-assumption (no mention of downstream review gate), cascade-check (search for callers before deletion), no-change-categorization (closed enum: CASCADE_RISK/INSUFFICIENT_CONTEXT/PATTERN_UNCLEAR/STACK_UNCERTAINTY), green-baseline (verify tests pass before any deletion), dynamic-reference-check (search for import()/require()/string refs/decorator registration), self-review (re-read own patches — check imports, calls, re-exports, __all__, type annotations), scope-creep-prohibition (no refactoring while cleaning).

Input/output contracts defined. Pattern-specific removal guidance for T1, T2, T3, C1, C2, D2, D4/D5/C3 included.

**Implementation must:** fill in pattern-specific edge case handling marked with {Implementation agents:} placeholders. Must NOT weaken, remove, or contradict any HARD-GATE section. The finality-assumption gate specifically prohibits adding context about downstream review — this is intentional (bot-psychologist finding: moral hazard).
comments.author: Test
